fact_summary: A music management company (Hummingbird Music Ltd) entered into exclusive agency and management agreements with two pop singers (the Accounci brothers, known as 'Soler'). The singers sought to rescind the agreements, claiming they were in restraint of trade and signed under undue influence. The Court of Appeal dismissed their arguments and upheld the contracts.
legal_question: Whether exclusive management agreements in the music industry constitute unreasonable restraint of trade under Hong Kong law.
hk_claim: The agreements were not in restraint of trade; they were normal commercial arrangements incidental to a joint venture. The ties were mutual and aligned with industry practice under the Copyright Ordinance (Cap. 528).
hk_legal_basis: Common law doctrine of restraint of trade

hk_sources.citation: Hummingbird Music Ltd v Accounci [2010] 1 HKLRD 587
Cap. 528 Copyright Ordinance

notes: Court of Appeal applied the two-stage test from Panayiotou v Sony Music Entertainment (UK) Ltd [1994] EMLR 229.

fact_summary: A financial institution issued a note containing an exclusion clause stating that no person shall have any right to enforce any term under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Ordinance (Cap. 623), but this does not affect rights existing apart from that Ordinance.
legal_question: Whether a contractual exclusion of third-party rights under Cap. 623 is valid and enforceable in Hong Kong.
hk_claim: Yes. Section 4(1) of Cap. 623 allows the parties to a contract to exclude the application of the Ordinance by express term. Such exclusion clauses are commonly used in financial instruments.
hk_legal_basis: Cap. 623 Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Ordinance, s.4(1)

hk_sources.citation: Cap. 623 Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Ordinance
ICBC Asia Deed Poll (2022)

notes: Cap. 623 came into force on 1 January 2016, reforming the common law privity rule.

fact_summary: A claimant suffered personal injury due to alleged negligence. The case involves the application of the common law duty of care and the principle of proximity in Hong Kong tort law.
legal_question: What is the test for establishing a duty of care in negligence under Hong Kong law?
hk_claim: Hong Kong follows the three-stage test from Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990] 2 AC 605: (1) foreseeability of damage, (2) proximity of relationship, and (3) whether it is fair, just and reasonable to impose a duty. This is applied consistently by the Hong Kong Court of Final Appeal.

hk_sources.citation: Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990] 2 AC 605 (PC/HK applied)
Common law negligence principles as applied in Hong Kong

notes: Hong Kong courts consistently apply the Caparo test for duty of care in negligence claims.

fact_summary: A professional firm (e.g., auditor or accountant) was sued by a company in liquidation for negligent misrepresentation causing economic loss. The issue concerns joint and several liability and whether proportionate liability should apply.
legal_question: Does Hong Kong recognize proportionate liability for professional negligence, or does joint and several liability apply?
hk_claim: Hong Kong currently maintains joint and several liability for tortious economic loss. The Law Reform Commission has considered proportionate liability but it has not been enacted as a general rule. Section 165 of the Companies Ordinance (Cap. 622) prohibits auditors from limiting liability by contract.
hk_legal_basis: Common law joint and several liability

hk_sources.citation: Cap. 622 Companies Ordinance, s.165
Law Reform Commission Report on Limited Liability Partnerships (2005)

notes: HKICPA has advocated for proportionate liability reform, but the general rule remains joint and several liability.

fact_summary: A Hong Kong incorporated private company sought to refuse registration of a share transfer. The directors refused on grounds that the instrument was not duly stamped or accompanied by the share certificate.
legal_question: Under what grounds may directors of a Hong Kong company refuse to register a transfer of shares?
hk_claim: Under the Companies Ordinance (Cap. 622) and standard articles of association, directors may refuse registration if: (a) the instrument of transfer is not lodged at the registered office; (b) it is not duly stamped; (c) it is not accompanied by the certificate; (d) the transfer is in respect of more than one class of shares; or (e) the company has a lien on the share.

hk_sources.citation: Cap. 622 Companies Ordinance
Re Mount Oscar Ltd [2019] 3 HKC 82

notes: Standard Table 2 Articles (private company) typically include these refusal grounds.

fact_summary: A minority shareholder of a Hong Kong company alleged oppression by the majority and sought a statutory derivative action under the Companies Ordinance.
legal_question: What remedies are available to minority shareholders in Hong Kong for unfair prejudice or oppression?
hk_claim: Under Cap. 622, s.724-725, a member may petition the court on the ground that the company's affairs are being conducted in a manner unfairly prejudicial to the interests of members. The court may make orders including regulating future conduct, purchase of shares, or winding up. Statutory derivative actions are available under Part 14 of Cap. 622.

hk_sources.citation: Cap. 622 Companies Ordinance, ss.724-725
Re Sit Kwong Lam (Debtor) [2019] 2 HKLRD 924

notes: The unfair prejudice remedy under Cap. 622 replaced the former common law 'oppression' remedy.

fact_summary: The defendants were charged with desecration of the national flag and regional flag contrary to s.7 of the National Flag and National Emblem Ordinance and s.7 of the Regional Flag and Regional Emblem Ordinance. They argued the provisions violated freedom of expression under the Basic Law and Bill of Rights.
legal_question: Whether criminalizing flag desecration is consistent with the constitutional guarantee of freedom of expression in Hong Kong.
hk_claim: Yes. The Court of Final Appeal held that the flag desecration provisions are a justified restriction on freedom of expression. The national flag is a unique symbol of the State's dignity, unity and territorial integrity. The restriction is 'prescribed by law' and pursues the legitimate aim of protecting national unity.
hk_legal_basis: Hong Kong Bill of Rights Ordinance (Cap. 383), Art.16
National Flag and National Emblem Ordinance (Cap. 1161), s.7

hk_sources.citation: HKSAR v Ng Kung Siu & Another (1999) 2 HKCFAR 442
National Flag and National Emblem Ordinance (Cap. 1161), s.7

notes: Landmark CFA decision on proportionality and freedom of expression restrictions.

fact_summary: A defendant was convicted of misconduct in public office. The offence was challenged as being too vague and uncertain, potentially violating the principle of legal certainty under Article 39 of the Basic Law.
legal_question: Is the common law offence of misconduct in public office unconstitutional for uncertainty under Hong Kong law?
hk_claim: No. The Court of Final Appeal saved the offence from unconstitutionality by interpreting it to require elements of limitation: the defendant must be a public officer, who in the course of or in relation to his public office, wilfully misconducts himself in a manner that is serious, without reasonable excuse or justification.
hk_legal_basis: Common law offence of misconduct in public office

hk_sources.citation: Shum Kwok Sher v HKSAR (2002) 5 HKCFAR 381
Basic Law, Art.39

notes: The CFA applied the principle of 'legal certainty' from international human rights jurisprudence.

fact_summary: An applicant sought leave to apply for judicial review of a government decision. The Court of First Instance dismissed the application on the ground that it was hypothetical and concerned a major constitutional issue without an actual case or specific factual background.
legal_question: What is the threshold for granting leave to apply for judicial review in Hong Kong?
hk_claim: The Court of Final Appeal raised the threshold in 2007 from 'potential arguability' to 'reasonable arguability' - whether the case enjoys realistic prospects of success. The court will not adjudicate hypothetical questions, especially on major constitutional issues, without an actual case or specific factual background.
hk_legal_basis: Basic Law, Art.35 (right to judicial remedies)
Rules of the High Court (Cap. 4A), Order 53

hk_sources.citation: Kwok Cheuk Kin v Chief Executive of HKSAR HCAL 1405/2020; [2020] HKCFI 1521
Leung Kwok Hung v Secretary for Justice [2020] HKCA 192

notes: Judicial review in Hong Kong adopts a two-stage approach: leave application (ex parte) and substantive hearing.

fact_summary: The Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) investigated a licensed corporation for suspected market misconduct and sought to exercise its statutory powers of investigation under the Securities and Futures Ordinance.
legal_question: What enforcement powers does the SFC have under the Securities and Futures Ordinance (Cap. 571) to investigate market misconduct?
hk_claim: Under Cap. 571, the SFC has extensive investigation powers including requiring production of records, requiring attendance for interview, and imposing civil penalties for market misconduct. The SFC may also apply to the Market Misconduct Tribunal for disciplinary orders.
hk_legal_basis: Cap. 571 Securities and Futures Ordinance, Part IX (investigations) and Part XIII (market misconduct)

hk_sources.citation: Cap. 571 Securities and Futures Ordinance
Secretary for Justice v Chow Yong Kang Alex [2018] HKCFA 4

notes: Cap. 571 modernized Hong Kong's securities regulation and came into effect in April 2003.

fact_summary: A data user transferred personal data of Hong Kong residents to a cloud server located outside Hong Kong for storage and processing. The data subjects were not informed or asked for consent.
legal_question: Is cross-border transfer of personal data from Hong Kong regulated under the PDPO, and what are the requirements?
hk_claim: Under Cap. 486 (PDPO), section 33 provides that personal data shall not be transferred to a place outside Hong Kong unless specified conditions are met. However, as of the current date, section 33 has not yet been brought into force. Pending activation, data users should still comply with Data Protection Principle 1 (purpose and manner of collection) and Principle 3 (use of personal data). Best practice requires obtaining consent and ensuring adequate protection in the destination jurisdiction.

hk_sources.citation: Cap. 486 Personal Data (Privacy) Ordinance
Privacy Commissioner for Personal Data Guidance on Cross-border Data Transfer

notes: Section 33 PDPO was enacted in 2012 but has not been brought into force as of 2024.

fact_summary: A company used personal data of customers for direct marketing purposes without obtaining their consent, in contravention of the PDPO amendment requirements.
legal_question: What are the consent requirements for direct marketing under the Hong Kong PDPO?
hk_claim: Under the PDPO (Cap. 486) as amended in 2012, data users must obtain express consent (opt-in) from data subjects before using their personal data for direct marketing. The consent must be informed, voluntary and specific. Data users must also provide an opt-out mechanism. Non-compliance may result in enforcement notices and fines up to HK$1,000,000 and imprisonment for 5 years for serious offences.
hk_legal_basis: Cap. 486 Personal Data (Privacy) Ordinance, Part VIA (direct marketing)

hk_sources.citation: Cap. 486 Personal Data (Privacy) Ordinance, Part VIA
Privacy Commissioner enforcement actions

notes: The 2012 Amendment Bill strengthened direct marketing regulation, effective from 1 April 2013.

fact_summary: A Hong Kong registered company was accused of trademark infringement by an international brand that had registered its trademark in Hong Kong. The defendant argued that the trademark was already successfully challenged in Mainland China.
legal_question: Does a Mainland China trademark judgment affect the validity and enforcement of a Hong Kong registered trademark?
hk_claim: No. Hong Kong and Mainland China maintain separate trademark registration systems under 'one country, two systems'. A judgment in Mainland China does not automatically affect a Hong Kong registered trademark. The trademark owner may still enforce its Hong Kong registration in Hong Kong courts. The Arrangement on Reciprocal Recognition and Enforcement of Judgments (2019) does not cover the validity of intellectual property rights.
hk_legal_basis: Mainland Judgments in Civil and Commercial Matters (Reciprocal Enforcement) Ordinance (Cap. 645)

hk_sources.citation: Cap. 559 Trade Marks Ordinance
Cap. 645 Mainland Judgments Reciprocal Enforcement Ordinance
Legislative Council debate on trademark cross-border enforcement (2020)

notes: Hong Kong maintains its own IP registration system independent of Mainland China.

fact_summary: A music producer acquired copyright in artists' recordings under standard industry agreements. The artists later claimed the copyright assignment was unconscionable and in restraint of trade.
legal_question: Are standard copyright assignments in music recording contracts valid under Hong Kong law?
hk_claim: Yes. Under the Copyright Ordinance (Cap. 528), it is conventional and workable for a producer to acquire copyright in artists' recordings in exchange for promotion and investment. The Court of Appeal in Hummingbird Music held that such arrangements are normal industry practice and do not constitute unreasonable restraint of trade.
hk_legal_basis: Common law doctrine of restraint of trade

hk_sources.citation: Hummingbird Music Ltd v Accounci [2010] 1 HKLRD 587
Cap. 528 Copyright Ordinance

notes: Copyright in sound recordings is governed by Cap. 528, which aligns with international standards.

fact_summary: An employee with 24 months of continuous service was dismissed by her employer. She claimed the dismissal was unreasonable and sought reinstatement or compensation.
legal_question: What remedies are available to an employee for unreasonable dismissal under the Hong Kong Employment Ordinance?
hk_claim: Under Cap. 57, an employee employed under a continuous contract for not less than 24 months may claim remedies for unreasonable dismissal. The Labour Tribunal may order: (1) reinstatement or re-engagement (with mutual consent); or (2) an award of terminal payments. For unreasonable and unlawful dismissal, the Tribunal may order reinstatement without employer consent (if reasonably practicable) and compensation up to HK$150,000.
hk_legal_basis: Cap. 57 Employment Ordinance, Part VIIA (Employment Protection)

hk_sources.citation: Cap. 57 Employment Ordinance
Labour Tribunal precedents on unreasonable dismissal

notes: The Employment (Amendment) (No.2) Ordinance 2018 strengthened remedies for unreasonable and unlawful dismissal, effective 19 October 2018.

fact_summary: An employer varied the terms of an employment contract without the employee's consent, reducing working hours and pay. The employee had been employed under a continuous contract.
legal_question: Can an employee claim remedies for unreasonable variation of employment contract terms under Hong Kong law?
hk_claim: Yes. Under Cap. 57, an employee employed under a continuous contract may claim remedies if: (a) the terms are varied without consent and the contract does not contain an express term allowing such variation; and (b) the variation is not for a valid reason. The Labour Tribunal may order reinstatement/re-engagement (with mutual consent) or terminal payments.

hk_sources.citation: Cap. 57 Employment Ordinance
Labour Department Concise Guide to Employment Protection

notes: Valid reasons for dismissal/variation include: conduct, capability, redundancy, statutory requirements, or other substantial reasons.

fact_summary: The HKSAR Government enacted the Safeguarding National Security Ordinance in March 2024 to implement Article 23 of the Basic Law, prohibiting acts of treason, secession, sedition, subversion, and theft of state secrets.
legal_question: What is the constitutional basis and scope of the Safeguarding National Security Ordinance in Hong Kong?
hk_claim: Article 23 of the Basic Law requires the HKSAR to enact laws on its own to prohibit seven types of acts endangering national security. The Safeguarding National Security Ordinance (enacted 23 March 2024) fulfills this constitutional duty. It is subordinate to the National Security Law (HKNSL) enacted by the NPCSC. The legislation must balance national security with protection of rights and freedoms under Basic Law Chapter 3 and Article 39 (ICCPR).

hk_sources.citation: Basic Law, Art.23
HKSAR v Ng Kung Siu (1999) 2 HKCFAR 442
Safeguarding National Security: Basic Law Article 23 Legislation Public Consultation Document (Jan 2024)

notes: The Ordinance was unanimously passed by LegCo on 19 March 2024 and took effect on 23 March 2024.

fact_summary: A solicitor challenged a requirement to disclose client communications to the Law Society during a disciplinary investigation, arguing it violated legal professional privilege guaranteed under Article 35 of the Basic Law.
legal_question: Does Article 35 of the Basic Law guarantee legal professional privilege as a constitutional right in Hong Kong?
hk_claim: Yes. Article 35 guarantees Hong Kong residents the right to confidential legal advice. The Court of Final Appeal and lower courts have recognized legal professional privilege (LPP) as a fundamental right constitutionally protected under Article 35. However, LPP is subject to exceptions and can be waived by the client. In disciplinary proceedings, the Law Society's need to maintain professional standards may justify limited disclosure, but the court will balance this against the constitutional right.

hk_sources.citation: FACV 8/2023 (Legal Professional Privilege case)
Shum Kwok Sher v HKSAR (2002) 5 HKCFAR 381
Basic Law, Art.35

notes: The CFA in FACV 8/2023 held that LPP is a fundamental right but subject to exceptions and waiver.

fact_summary: A party to civil litigation in the High Court sought discovery of documents from the opposing party, including documents that might be subject to legal professional privilege.
legal_question: What is the scope of discovery and disclosure in Hong Kong civil procedure, and how is legal professional privilege protected?
hk_claim: Under the High Court Rules (Cap. 4A), Order 24, parties must disclose all documents relevant to matters in question, except privileged documents. Legal professional privilege (both advice privilege and litigation privilege) is a recognized exception. The court may order specific discovery if satisfied that the party has failed to make proper discovery. The court will balance the need for full disclosure against the protection of confidential legal communications.

hk_sources.citation: High Court Rules (Cap. 4A), Order 24
Three Rivers District Council v Bank of England (No.6) [2005] 1 AC 610 (applied in HK)

notes: Hong Kong civil discovery follows the English model under the RSC, with ongoing reforms toward CPR-style disclosure.

fact_summary: A litigant sought to enforce a Mainland China civil judgment in Hong Kong. The judgment concerned a commercial contract dispute.
legal_question: Under what conditions can a Mainland China judgment be enforced in Hong Kong?
hk_claim: Under the Mainland Judgments in Civil and Commercial Matters (Reciprocal Enforcement) Ordinance (Cap. 645), effective 29 January 2024, a judgment from a designated Mainland court may be registered and enforced in Hong Kong if: (a) it is an effective judgment; (b) it falls within the scope of 'civil and commercial matters'; (c) it is given by a court listed in the Schedule; and (d) it satisfies the jurisdictional and procedural requirements. The defendant may challenge registration on limited grounds including lack of jurisdiction, fraud, or public policy.
hk_legal_basis: Cap. 645 Mainland Judgments in Civil and Commercial Matters (Reciprocal Enforcement) Ordinance

hk_sources.citation: Cap. 645 Mainland Judgments in Civil and Commercial Matters (Reciprocal Enforcement) Ordinance
Legislative Council debate on Cap. 645 (2022)

notes: Cap. 645 replaced the former Arrangement with a statutory registration regime, significantly expanding the scope of enforceable judgments.
